evidence_refs.quote: # Replay real captured agent traces with repository-scale snapshot binding

"claim_scope": "On 6,247 read-only shell command events from 775 local real Enoch/Codex trace logs, binding commands to their originating project workspace improves exact exit+output replay from 18.23% under a current-workspace baseline and 17.62% under a shuffled-project control to 58.11%.",

"claim_scope": "On 6,247 read-only shell command events from 775 local real Enoch/Codex trace logs, binding commands to their originating project workspace improves exact exit+output replay from 18.23% under a current-workspace baseline and 17.62% under a shuffled-project control to 58.11%.",

claims.notes: Matched by lexical overlap against synced worker artifacts.

claims.claim: The operator of the research system claims no personal authorship credit for either the writing or the empirical results, beyond releasing the artifacts into the public bundle.

evidence_refs.quote: Hypothesis tested: captured agent shell traces become substantially more replayable when each command is bound back to its originating repository/workspace snapshot, compared with replaying the same command from a generic current workspace or a mismatched repository.

"scale_limits": "The validation uses local Enoch traces only, excludes mutating/install/network commands, replays shell commands rather than full agent decisions, and binds to final project workspaces rather than historical pre-command filesystem snapshots.",

claims.claim: Per-command-family diagnostics show large lift for `sed`, `jq`, `wc`, and `tail`, but only 13.74% exact match for `find` under origin binding, indicating that final-snapshot repository binding alone does not reconstruct historical file inventories after later workspace drift.

claims.claim: The project decision for this run is `finalize_negative`: the result is a useful, bounded empirical signal that origin binding is a necessary component of faithful shell-trace replay, but final-snapshot binding leaves 41.89% of commands non-exact and is therefore not sufficient for high-fidelity repository-scale replay.

claims.claim: A natural first hypothesis is that the dominant source of replay non-determinism is the file and project state in which each command runs: a `find`, `rg`, `wc`, or `sed` issued inside one repository will produce a different result if executed from a different repository, or from a "current" workspace that has been mutated by later artifacts.
